Swiss CitiesBurgdorf

Burgdorf

BEBern

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The red castle on the hill

A child discovers that the red bricks of Schloss Burgdorf were unlike anything the medieval builders had ever seen. Through a magic brick, they travel back to the year 1200 and help Duke Berchtold V build his castle, learning why he chose this bold new material that would make the fortress glow red above the Emme for eight centuries.

Create This Story

Burgdorf Castle is the largest and best-preserved Zaehringen castle complex in Switzerland, classified as a historical monument of national significance. Around 1200, Duke Berchtold V of Zaehringen rebuilt the castle in monumental form using red brick -- a material unfamiliar in the region at that time -- giving it a distinctive majestic red appearance. The complex stretches 130 metres along a sandstone rock formation above the Emme river and includes a residential tower (Palas), a fortified tower (Bergfried), and a great hall connecting the two.

Pestalozzi's wildest pupil

A restless child arrives at Burgdorf Castle in 1801 expecting punishment, but finds a white-haired teacher who throws away all the textbooks and says 'First we think, then we read.' Together they discover learning through head, hand, and heart -- and the child realizes that the greatest classroom in Switzerland is a medieval castle.

Create This Story

Johann Heinrich Pestalozzi (1746-1827), the most famous educator in Europe, arrived in Burgdorf in 1799. His revolutionary methods abolished textbooks, emphasised sensory learning, and taught 'the powers of head, hand, and heart.' In October 1800, he moved into Burgdorf Castle itself, establishing his Educational Institute combining a boys' school, a school for the poor, and a teacher-training forum. Hundreds of educators and politicians from across Europe visited to witness his methods. In 1804, the government of Bern demanded he vacate the castle.

The 45-day siege

During the Burgdorferkrieg of 1383, a child trapped inside the besieged castle must find a secret passage along the Emme river to carry a message to the outside world. Over 45 harrowing days, they discover courage, witness the true cost of war, and learn how the sale of a castle for 37,800 guilders changed the fate of Switzerland.

Create This Story

The Burgdorferkrieg (Burgdorf War) of 1383-1384 began when Count Rudolf II of Neu-Kyburg launched a reckless raid on Solothurn in November 1382 to force the city to forgive his debts. For Bern, this was the perfect pretext to break the Kyburgs' power. In March 1383, Bern attacked Burgdorf. Under the Kyburg commander Berchtold, the castle withstood a 45-day siege. On 5 April 1384, the Neu-Kyburgs sold Burgdorf and Thun to Bern for 37,800 guilders -- a decisive step in Bern's rise to become the most powerful Swiss city-state.

The cheese with the mysterious holes

A child on a school trip to an Emmental show dairy accidentally shrinks to the size of a bacterium and travels inside a wheel of Emmentaler. From the inside, they watch the tiny Propionibacterium at work, blowing carbon dioxide bubbles that become the famous holes, and must find their way out before the cheese is sealed for its months of aging.

Create This Story

Emmentaler (known internationally as 'Swiss cheese') originated on the alpine pastures of the Emme valley surrounding Burgdorf. The name first appeared in 1542, though cheese production in the Canton of Bern dates to 1293. The characteristic large holes are caused by Propionibacterium, which consume proteins in the curd during maturation -- the carbon dioxide they produce becomes trapped, forming spherical cavities. Emmentaler received Swiss AOP status in 2000. Over 20,000 tonnes are produced annually in the region's show dairies along the Kaeseroute (Cheese Route).

The gateway and the giant

Standing between the castle hill and the Gysnaufluehe cliffs, a child realizes the two rock formations are ancient sleeping giants who once guarded the entrance to the Emmental. When the Emme rises to its highest level in 300 years, the giants stir in their sleep, and the child must convince them the valley is still worth protecting.

Create This Story

Burgdorf sits in a meander of the Emme river at its confluence with the Ilfis. The commanding castle hill to the west and the striking Gysnaufluehe cliff formation to the east together form the iconic 'gateway' -- earning Burgdorf its title as the 'Tor zum Emmental' (Gateway to the Emmental). The Emme is an 80-kilometre river rising between the peaks of Hohgant (2,197 m) and Augstmatthorn (2,137 m) in the Bernese Alps. In July 2014, an exceptionally severe flood caused the Emme to reach its highest level in 300 years.

The Hornuss that flew too far

At a Hornussen match near Burgdorf, a child's strike sends the puck soaring beyond the field and over the rolling Emmental hills at 300 km/h. Chasing after the buzzing hornet through farmsteads and cheese dairies, the child traces the sport's origins all the way back to 1625, when two men were fined for playing on a Sunday.

Create This Story

Hornussen originated in the Emmental region, with the earliest documented reference in 1625 from the consistory of Lauperswil (Canton of Bern), where two men were fined 20 francs for playing on Sunday. The first recorded competitive match took place in 1655 in Trub. A striking team hits a puck (the 'Hornuss' or 'hornet') from a flexible ramp at up to 300 km/h, producing a buzzing sound, while the defending team of 16-20 players intercepts it with large wooden boards called Schindeln. The Emmental is the historic heartland of both Hornussen and Schwingen (Swiss wrestling).

The electric spark

In 1899, a child boards the very first electric train from Burgdorf to Thun, marvelling as the locomotive glides silently where horses once strained. At each station, Professor Blattner explains how water from the Kander river became lightning in the wires, and the child witnesses the dawn of a new age on Europe's first fully electric railway.

Create This Story

On 22 July 1899, the Burgdorf-Thun Railway opened as the first fully electric standard-gauge railway line in Europe. The 41-kilometre line was electrified with 750 volts three-phase AC at 40 hertz, with trains running at a maximum speed of 36 km/h. The project was driven by Emil Blattner, professor of electrical engineering at the Burgdorf College of Technology and city councillor. Electrical equipment was supplied by Brown, Boveri & Cie. (later ABB), with power from the Kander hydroelectric station near Spiez on Lake Thun. The total cost was 5.4 million Swiss francs.

Night at the castle hostel

A child staying overnight in the Burgdorf Castle youth hostel hears footsteps echoing through the old corridors. Following them leads to encounters with Pestalozzi's ghost students reciting lessons, a Kyburg knight polishing his armour, and finally Duke Berchtold himself, each sharing one lesson about what the castle has witnessed over 800 years.

Create This Story

After a 17-million-franc renovation (2017-2020), Burgdorf Castle reopened on 26 April 2020 as a combined museum, youth hostel (31 rooms, 115 beds), restaurant, and civil wedding venue. In 2021, the renovation was honoured with the Atuprix (audience award for exemplary Bernese architecture) and ICOMOS Switzerland's 'Historic Hotel of the Year 2022' award. The castle has served continuously as an administrative seat for over seven centuries, from the Kyburg counts to the Bernese Schultheiss (municipal governor) from 1384, and as a courthouse until 2012.
